title: Richard Werner: Quantity Theory of Credit

cells.title: Overview
cells.source:
# Richard Werner's Quantity Theory of Credit

Werner's key distinction is not simply the quantity of money, but the destination of newly created bank credit.

- Productive credit finances GDP transactions: production, wages, inventories, and investment.
- Asset credit finances existing assets: land, housing, shares, and speculative financial assets.

In this notebook, banks create two credit circulations. Credit for GDP transactions drives nominal output. Credit for financial transactions drives the total value of asset-market turnover and therefore the asset-price index.

cells.title: QTC equations
cells.source:
## Quantity Theory of Credit equations

Werner's Quantity Theory of Credit splits the quantity equation into two separate circuits:

- Real-economy credit: `Cr * Vr = P * Yr = Y`
- Financial credit: `Cf * Vf = PA * AQty = PAQ`

`Cr` is bank credit used for GDP transactions and `Vr` is its velocity. `Cf` is credit used for financial transactions and `Vf` is its velocity. With velocity treated as relatively stable, growth in `Cr` maps to nominal GDP growth, while growth in `Cf` maps to asset-price inflation.

cells.title: Main variables
cells.source:
| Variable | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| `Y` | nominal GDP / income | money per period |
| `C` | consumption | money per period |
| `I` | productive investment | money per period |
| `Cr` | bank credit used for GDP transactions | money per period |
| `Cf` | bank credit used for financial transactions | money per period |
| `Vr` | velocity of real-economy credit | turns per period |
| `Vf` | velocity of financial credit | turns per period |
| `Dp` | change in productive credit | money per period |
| `Da` | change in asset credit | money per period |
| `Lp` | stock of productive loans | money |
| `La` | stock of asset loans | money |
| `M` | deposits | money |
| `PA` | asset price index | index |
| `PAQ` | total value of asset transactions | money per period |
| `W` | household wealth | money |
| `DebtRatio` | total loans relative to GDP | ratio |

cells.title: Balance sheet: loan-created deposits
cells.description: Balance-sheet view showing how bank-created credit maps into deposits, loans, and asset holdings.

cells.title: Transaction matrix: productive vs asset credit
cells.description: Transaction-flow view separating credit directed to current production from credit directed to existing assets.

cells.title: Transaction flow sequence
cells.description: Sequence view generated from the productive-versus-asset-credit transaction matrix.

equations.name: gp
equations.desc: Growth rate of credit used for GDP transactions.

equations.name: ga
equations.desc: Growth rate of credit used for financial transactions.

equations.name: Vr
equations.desc: Velocity of circulation for real-economy credit.

equations.name: Vf
equations.desc: Velocity of circulation for financial credit.

equations.name: Cr
equations.desc: Bank credit circulating in GDP transactions.

equations.name: Cf
equations.desc: Bank credit circulating in financial transactions.

equations.name: Y
equations.desc: Nominal GDP from the real-economy credit equation Cr * Vr = P * Yr.

equations.name: PAQ
equations.desc: Total value of asset transactions from the financial-credit equation Cf * Vf = PA * AQty.

equations.name: PA
equations.desc: Asset price index implied by financial-credit turnover and the quantity of assets traded.

equations.name: Dp
equations.desc: Change in credit circulating through GDP transactions.

equations.name: Da
equations.desc: Change in credit circulating through financial transactions.

equations.name: Lp
equations.desc: Stock of productive loans.

equations.name: La
equations.desc: Stock of asset loans.

equations.name: L
equations.desc: Total bank loans.

equations.name: I
equations.desc: Productive investment and other non-consumption GDP demand.

equations.name: C
equations.desc: Consumption demand.

equations.name: M
equations.desc: Deposits created by net new credit in both circulations.

equations.name: PAStock
equations.desc: Value of existing assets held by households.

equations.name: W
equations.desc: Household wealth net of asset-credit liabilities.

equations.name: DebtRatio
equations.desc: Total loans relative to GDP.

equations.name: ProductiveCreditShare
equations.desc: Share of circulating bank credit used for GDP transactions.

equations.name: AssetCreditShare
equations.desc: Share of circulating bank credit used for financial transactions.

equations.name: RealCreditGrowth
equations.desc: Growth form for real-economy credit.

equations.name: FinancialCreditGrowth
equations.desc: Growth form for financial credit.

equations.name: NominalGDPGrowth
equations.desc: Growth rate of nominal GDP implied by real-economy credit growth.

equations.name: AssetPriceGrowth
equations.desc: Growth rate of asset prices implied by financial-credit growth.

equations.name: BankNW
equations.desc: Illustrative banking-sector net worth implied by loans minus deposits.

externals.name: alpha1
externals.desc: Marginal propensity to consume out of lagged income.

externals.name: alpha2
externals.desc: Marginal propensity to consume out of lagged wealth.

externals.name: rp
externals.desc: Interest rate on productive loans, used in the accounting view.

externals.name: ra
externals.desc: Interest rate on asset loans, used in the accounting view.

externals.name: Vr_s
externals.desc: Velocity of circulation for GDP transactions.

externals.name: Vf_s
externals.desc: Velocity of circulation for financial transactions.

externals.name: gp_s
externals.desc: Scenario control for real-economy credit growth.

cells.title: Baseline: more productive credit
cells.description: Baseline run with productive credit growth stronger than asset-credit growth.

cells.title: Scenario 1
cells.source: This scenario shifts credit creation toward asset purchases by lowering real-economy credit growth and raising financial-credit growth. In Werner's framing, this is the credit pattern associated with asset-price inflation rather than broad GDP acceleration.

cells.title: Asset boom: more asset credit
cells.description: Higher asset-credit growth should push asset prices and leverage above the baseline path.

cells.title: Scenario 2
cells.source: This scenario slows both credit circulations to show how weaker bank lending compresses nominal GDP growth and cools asset-price growth.

cells.title: Credit crunch: falling credit growth
cells.description: Lower credit growth should weaken output and reduce the pace of asset-price and leverage growth.

cells.title: Equation dependency graph
cells.description: Dependency view showing how credit allocation, income, deposits, asset prices, and leverage interact.

cells.title: Interpretation
cells.source:
## Interpretation

The model separates bank credit into two flows. Productive credit, `Dp`, directly finances investment and enters GDP demand. Asset credit, `Da`, does not directly create current production. Instead it raises the asset-price index through the asset-credit-pressure term.

That captures Werner's central point: credit for real-economy transactions tends to raise GDP, while credit for existing assets tends to raise asset prices and leverage.
